coredata.dc:title: Flammulina velutipes-derived carbon dots for fluorescence detection and imaging of hydroxyl radical
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Hou, Yu
Lin, Jiawei
Chen, Yichao
He, Jianbin
Su, Zhe
Zheng, Qinhua
Zhao, Xiujuan
Lv, Xiaodan
Tang, Xiuping
Zhou, Changren
coredata.dc:description: Monitoring hydroxyl radical (•OH) fluctuation is of great importance to study some relative pathological processes and to predict early diagnosis of diseases. Efficient •OH-responsive fluorescent sensors based on carbon dots (CDs) have been reported, but most researches have focused on the new strategies for the synthesis and doping of the CDs. Herein, a kind of biomass CDs (F-CDs) with Flammulina velutipes (F. velutipes) as the carbon source was prepared by a one-step hydrothermal method without any additional modification. The prepared F-CDs have remarkable sensitivity and selectivity and there is a good linear relationship from 0 to 12 μM with a low detection limit of 95 nM for quantitative •OH assay. With excitation-independent emission, favourable biocompatibility and low toxicity, the F-CDs can penetrate cell membranes as •OH-responsive fluorescent sensors to detect intracellular •OH in A549 cells stimulated by phorbol 12-myristate 13-acetate (PMA) and successfully monitor the •OH concentration levels by the corresponding fluorescence change. Given the combined benefits of the green and eco-friendly approach, the F-CDs show promise as novel theranostics tools for early detection and treatment of related diseases.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescence sensor
Hydroxyl radical
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Stability studies of the F-CDs in the presence of different ions and ROS
Morphology and characterizations of the F-CDs
Detection of the •OH in living cells

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The study was supported by the Innovative School Project of Education Department of Guangdong (No. 2018KTSCX305, 2021KQNCX145, and 2022KTSCX187), the Scientific and Technological Innovation Strategy of Guangdong Province (“Climbing Plan”) Special Fund Project in 2022 (No. pdjh2022b0723), the Key Construction Academic Subject for Scientific Research Quality Improvement of Guangdong Province (No. 2021ZDJS139) and Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Fund Regional Joint Youth Fund (No. 2019A515110262).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphology and characterization of the F-CDs. a) TEM image of the F-CDs (inset: the size distribution of the F-CDs); b) HRTEM image of the F-CDs; c) XRD spectrum of the F-CDs; d) FTIR spectrum of the F-CDs; e) XPS spectrum of the F-CDs; f)-h) High resolution XPS spectrum and fitting results of f) C 1s, g) O 1s and h) N 1s.

A) uv–vis absorption spectrum, pl spectrum of the f-cds (insets are photographs of the f-cds under visible and uv (365 nm) light); b) PL spectra of the F-CDs at different excitation wavelengths. (2D representation).

A) fluorescence responses of the f-cds toward different metal ions (1 mM Fe2+, 1 mM Cu2+, 1 mM K+, 1 mM Ca2+, 1 mM Mn2+, 1 mM Ni2+) and various ROS (1 mM TBHP, 1 mM ClO−, 1 mM H2O2, 1 mM 1O2 (1 mM H2O2 + 1 mM OCl-) and 30 μM •OH (30 μM H2O2 + 30 μM Fe2+-EDTA)); b) PL spectra of different reaction systems: F-CDs only (black); F-CDs + 30 μM H2O2 (red); F-CDs + 30 μM Fe2+-EDTA (blue); F-CDs + 30 μM Fe2+-EDTA + 30 μM H2O2 (purple); c) Fluorescence responses of the F-CDs to the •OH with different concentrations (0–12 μM); d) Plot of c) fluorescence quenching rate ((F0-F)/F0) of the F-CDs as a function of the •OH concentrations (Inset: the linear relationship (R2 = 0.996) between the fluorescence quenching rate and concentration of the •OH). The above samples were examined in PBS solution (pH = 7.4) at the excitation wavelength of 380 nm for 10 min at 25 ℃.

A) lifetime of f-cds (purple) and f-cds + 30 μM •OH (blue); b) UV–vis absorption spectrum of the F-CDs toward different concentrations of the •OH; c) CV curves of the GCE electrode modified with the F-CDs and after treated with 1 mM Fe2+ or/and 6 mM H2O2. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Cell viability of A549 cells and HepG2 cells with the F-CDs at different concentrations (0, 10, 20, 30, 40 and 50 μg/mL) for 24 h, respectively.

Fluorescence microscopic images of The A549 cells were pretreated with the F-CDs (20 μg/mL) for 24 h and then treated with a) 0 μM; b) 2 μM; c) 4 μM and d) 8 μM of •OH for 30 min. e) Relative pixel intensity of the fluorescence images in a)-d). The fluorescence images were collected at a 340 nm excitation wavelength. Scale bar is 50 μm.

Fluorescence microscopic images of a) The A549 cells were pretreated with the F-CDs (20 μg/mL) for 24 h; b) and c) The A549 cells were pretreated with the F-CDs (20 μg/mL) for 24 h and then stimulated with PMA (0.5 μg/mL) for b) 2 h and c) 4 h.; d) The A549 cells were pretreated with the F-CDs (20 μg/mL) for 24 h, then with TEMPO (1 mM) for 1 h and finally stimulated with PMA (0.5 μg/mL) for 4 h; e) Relative pixel intensity of the fluorescence images in a)-d). The fluorescence images were collected at a 340 nm excitation wavelength. Scale bar is 50 μm.
Schematic illustration of the preparation of F-CDs for the sensing and intracellular imaging of •OH.
Comparison of different fluorescent sensors with some previous literatures for the •OH detection.

head.ce:title: Flammulina velutipes-derived carbon dots for fluorescence detection and imaging of hydroxyl radical

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Applied Chemistry and Materials, Zhuhai College of Science and Technology, Zhuhai 519040, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Applied Chemistry and Materials
Zhuhai College of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy and Food Sciences, Zhuhai College of Science and Technology, Zhuhai 519040, PR China

Zhuhai College of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy and Food Sciences, Zhuhai College of Science and Technology, Zhuhai 519040, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Bioengineering, Zunyi Medical University Zhuhai Campus, Zhuhai 519041, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Bioengineering, Zunyi Medical University Zhuhai Campus, Zhuhai 519041, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A kind of biomass CDs (F-CDs) with the polysaccharide-rich precursor of Flammulina velutipes (F. velutipes) through a simple one-step hydrothermal approach, are used as fluorescent •OH-responsive sensors for sensing and intracellular imaging of •OH.

Monitoring hydroxyl radical (•OH) fluctuation is of great importance to study some relative pathological processes and to predict early diagnosis of diseases. Efficient •OH-responsive fluorescent sensors based on carbon dots (CDs) have been reported, but most researches have focused on the new strategies for the synthesis and doping of the CDs. Herein, a kind of biomass CDs (F-CDs) with Flammulina velutipes (F. velutipes) as the carbon source was prepared by a one-step hydrothermal method without any additional modification. The prepared F-CDs have remarkable sensitivity and selectivity and there is a good linear relationship from 0 to 12 μM with a low detection limit of 95 nM for quantitative •OH assay. With excitation-independent emission, favourable biocompatibility and low toxicity, the F-CDs can penetrate cell membranes as •OH-responsive fluorescent sensors to detect intracellular •OH in A549 cells stimulated by phorbol 12-myristate 13-acetate (PMA) and successfully monitor the •OH concentration levels by the corresponding fluorescence change. Given the combined benefits of the green and eco-friendly approach, the F-CDs show promise as novel theranostics tools for early detection and treatment of related diseases.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Reactive oxygen species (ROS) as a class of reactive species are mainly generated by normal oxygen metabolism via electron transfer reactions and widely acknowledged to play significant roles in cell survival, proliferation, differentiation, death, apoptosis etc [1–3]. Among them, hydroxyl radicals (•OH) have received increasing attention because of their extremely strong oxidisability, with a high electrode reduction potential of +2.31 V. Different levels of •OH influence some physiological and pathological processes. However, excessive •OH will damage the DNA, proteins, lipids and cells and increase the risk of the oxidative stress-related diseases, such as aging, rheumatic arthritis, cancer and Parkinson’s diseases [4,5]. Developing endogenous •OH sensors can provide extremely important information for various cellular functions, pathological processes and the early diagnosis and treatment of diseases.

Among many analytical methods, fluorescence probe technology is considered highly suitable for the detection of ROS due to its high sensitivity, visual features, convenience, spatiotemporal resolution and excellent biocompatibility [6,7]. However, compared with the fluorescent sensors for other ROS [8–11], the development of the fluorescent •OH sensors is slow, and challenges exist due to the low concentration, considerable reactivity and exceedingly short lifetime (∼1 ns) of •OH [5,12]. To date, the research strategies reported on detecting •OH have been primarily based on small organic molecule fluorescent sensors [13–17] and nanoparticle (NP) fluorescence sensors (such as polymeric NPs [18,19], quantum dots [20,21], metal nanoclusters [22,23], upconversion NPs [24,25] etc.). Two approaches are commonly used for NP-based fluorescence sensors. Non-emissive NPs are used as carriers for molecular fluorescent dyes or fluorescent NPs, and molecular dyes are assembled as fluorescent couples for multi-channel detection. Despite the potential of the sensors for monitoring exogenous or endogenous •OH, many difficulties remain, including insufficient sensitivity, complex preparation process and toxic organic dyes and heavy metals, which limit the research range [5].

Carbon dots (CDs), as quasi-spherical luminescent carbon-based NPs (<10 nm), have attracted the attention of many researchers in bioimaging and chemical sensing because of their fascinating characteristics of excellent biocompatibility, convenient functionalisation, low toxicity and outstanding optical properties [26–29]. CDs with abundant edge/defect sites come from numerous surface groups, and their π-conjugated nature can easily provide an extremely high H donation ability and promote the charge transfer process between ROS and the CDs [30,31]. CDs have been proven promising as a sensor platform in detecting ROS, including superoxide (O2 •−) [32], hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) [33,34], singlet state (1O2) [35] and •OH [21,36]. Overall, the focus of most efforts has been on the new strategies for CD synthesis and doping. With the increasing environmental consciousness of people, biomass-derived CDs are gaining popularity because of their easy preparation, extensive resources, green synthesis, low cost and environmental friendliness [37]. Moreover, CDs are endowed with abundant surface functional groups, like hydroxyl, carboxyl and carbonyl groups, which are derived from raw material sources for remarkable chemical and biological characteristics [37]. However, as far as we know, very few researches on •OH-responsive fluorescent sensors based on natural biomass CDs have been reported.

Flammulina velutipes (F. velutipes) is one of the most popular cultivated mushrooms in the world. Given its abundant substances, like polysaccharides, proteins, essential fatty acids and even several bioactive compounds, many studies have shown that F. velutipes has potentially health-promoting effects on anti-cancer, anti-oxidation, immunomodulatory activities etc [38–41].

Inspired by the above discoveries, we prepared a kind of biomass CDs (F-CDs) with F. velutipes as the natural carbon source, without any extra modification, through a simple one-step hydrothermal approach (Scheme 1 ). Compared with other ROS, the F-CDs exhibited prominent selectivity and sensitivity as efficient fluorescent sensors for the •OH generated from the Fenton reaction. Furthermore, a series of analyses was performed on the optical and physicochemical properties of them. Meanwhile, the redox behaviour between this kind of biomass CDs and the •OH was studied by electrochemical method. The F-CDs showed low cytotoxicity, good biocompatibility and an anti-jamming capability. On the basis of these excellent properties, the F-CDs were successfully applied to monitor the intracellular •OH via fluorescence imaging. This work exhibits the considerable potential of biomass-derived CDs as a new class of •OH sensors for environmental and biological applications for early detection and treatment of related diseases.

F. velutipes was acquired from Walmart in Zhuhai, China. Ferrous sulphate heptahydrate (FeSO4·7H2O), potassium chloride (KCl), calcium chloride (CaCl2), manganese sulphate monohydrate (MnSO4·H2O), lead nitrate (PbNO3), nickel sulphate hexahydrate (NiSO4·6H2O), sodium hypochlorite (NaClO), ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), tert-butyl hydroperoxide (TBHP), and quinine sulphate were bought from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Nafion®117 was bought from Sigma-Aldrich. Beyotime (Shanghai, China) provided a Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8). Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM), phorbol 12-myristate 13-acetate (PMA), 2,2,6,6-tetramethyl-1-piperidinyloxy (TEMPO) penicillin, streptomycin and phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) were bought from Hyclone (Thermo Scientific, USA). Procell (Wuhan, China) supplied the fetal bovine serum (FBS). The Chinese Academy of Sciences' cell bank (Shanghai, China) provided the A549 and HepG2 cell lines. Dialysis membranes (molecular weight cutoff = 1000) were obtained from Spectrum Labs (Shanghai, China). In addition to these, analytical grade chemicals were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Company Limited (Shanghai, China).

The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded on all samples using a RF-5301PC spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Japan) at 5/5 nm slit width. To minimise interference, the absorbance of the F-CDs solution was calibrated to 0.1 at 380 nm. The fluorescence intensity represents the maximum value of the emission wavelength, and the test data were normalised. An ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) was used to analyze the absorption spectra. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) was investigated using a Malvern Nano ZS instrument (Malvern, UK). For structural verification, the X-ray diffraction spectrum was recorded with an Ultima IV (Rigaku, Japan) instrument. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were analysed using an IR Prestige-21 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) with KBr pellets. The elemental and functional groups of the samples were determined with X-ray photoelectron spectrometry (XPS) on an ESCALAB 250XI surface analysis system (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). In order to measure high resolution transmission electron microscopic (HRTEM) images of the F-CDs, a JEM-2100 (HR) (JEOL, Japan) was used at a voltage of 200 KV. The fluorescence lifetime curves of the untreated and pretreated samples were obtained using a time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) system (Boston Electronic, USA) with a 405-nm excitation and a time resolution of 200 ps. A CHI 660E electrochemical workstation (Chenhua Instruments, China) was used for cyclic voltammetry (CV) experiments using a conventional three electrode system. Working electrode was a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) or a modified GCE electrode, reference electrode was an Ag/AgCl electrode (3.0 M KCl), and counter electrode was a platinum plate electrode A549 cells were imaged using an inverted fluorescent microscope (Nikon, Japan). The cytotoxicity experiments were analysed using a microplate reader (Tecan Sunrise, Switzerland).

The F-CDs were hydrothermally obtained with F. velutipes as the precursor material. The fresh F. velutipes was washed entirely with deionised water and placed into a vacuum drying chamber at 50 °C. Then, 2 g of F. velutipes and 10 mL of deionised water were smashed and mixed by a food processor. A Teflon-lined reactor was used to heat and maintain the reaction mixture for 8 h at 180 °C, and then naturally cool it to room temperature. Precipitated substances were removed with a centrifugal machine at 10000 rpm for 10 min, and insoluble carbonised particles were filtrated through filter membranes (0.1 μm). Finally, the deep yellow solution was iteratively subjected to dialysis with deionised water in some dialysis bags (1000 KDa) for 36 h to obtain a pure F-CDs solution for further characterisation.

At room temperature, fluorescence detection of the •OH in PBS solution (pH = 7.4) was carried out. All the fluorescence measurements were recorded at the 380-nm excitation wavelength (5/5 nm slit width). Fenton reaction is a widely used method for generating •OH. In the presence of Fe2+, the •OH generated from H2O2 was estimated to be quantitative. A series of •OH solutions with concentrations from 0 to 12 μM were introduced into the homogeneous system and kept for 10 min. Then, the PL spectra of the above samples were obtained under the 380-nm excitation wavelength.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability studies of the F-CDs in the presence of different ions and ROS
ce:section.ce:para: Different metal ions and ROS (i.e. Fe2+, Cu2+, K+, Ca2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, •OH, ClO-, H2O2,1O2 and TBHP) were separately added into 3-mL F-CDs solutions. Except for the concentration of the •OH (30 μM), the final concentrations of the metal ions and ROS mentioned above were kept at 1 mM. For minimal interference, the absorbance of the F-CDs solution was calibrated to 0.1 at 380 nm and the above samples were examined in the PBS solution (pH = 7.4) with a 10-min incubation step.

To fabricate the F-CDs modified electrode, a GCE was carefully polished to a mirror-like finish with Al2O3 (1.0, 0.3, and 0.05 μm) and rinsed several times with ethyl alcohol and deionised water. Then 10 μL of the F-CDs containing 0.5 % Nafion (v:v) was added slowly onto the surface of the bare GCE and dried with the aid of an infrared light. The GCE electrode modified with the F-CDs was obtained.

CV experiment were conducted in KCl solution (0.1 M) and the potential range was from −0.6 to 0.4 V vs Ag/AgCl with a scan rate of 100 mV s−1. The electrode modified with the F-CDs was treated with the •OH solutions (1 mM Fe2+ and 6 mM H2O2).and the corresponding CV curves were recorded. The CV curves of the F-CDs modified electrode treated with 1 mM Fe2+ or 6 mM H2O2 and the control electrode modified treated with the •OH (1 mM Fe2+ and 6 mM H2O2) were recorded using the same process. Each experiment was repeated three times.

The A549 cells were cultured at a density of 1 × 104 cell/well (37 °C, 5 % CO2) in 96-well plates with DMEM supplemented with 10 % FBS, 100 U/mL of penicillin and 100 mg/mL of streptomycin for 24 h. Subsequently, the cells were adherent and a fresh medium containing the F-CDs with different final concentrations (i.e. 0, 10, 20, 30, 40 and 50 μg/mL) was used to replace the medium. The cells were then washed three times with the PBS buffer after another 24 h of incubation. Next, 100 μL of DMEM containing 10 % CCK-8 dye was introduced to per well. Cell viability was measured at 450 nm after 2 h. According to the following equation, the viability of cells was determined [42]:
Cell
v i a b i l i t y
%
=
A 1
/
A 2
×
100 %
Where A1 and A2 are the absorbance of the wells with and without F-CDs, respectively. The standard deviations of the final results were calculated based on five times of the experiment. The cell viability of the HepG2 cells was obtained through a similar procedure as the above experiments.

For imaging various concentrations of the •OH in living cells, the A549 cells were pretreated with the F-CDs (20 μg/mL) for 24 h and the extra F-CDs were removed by washing the A549 cells three times with PBS (pH 7.4). Then, the cells were treated with H2O2 (1 mM) and various concentrations of Fe2+ (0, 2, 4, 8 μM) for 30 min. After three washes with the PBS solution (pH = 7.4), the cells were imaged with an inverted fluorescent microscope at 340-nm excitation wavelength. For imaging the endogenous •OH, the A549 cells in the experimental groups were first incubated with the fresh medium containing 20 μg/mL of the F-CDs for 24 h and then pretreated with 0.5 μg/mL of PMA at 37 °C for 0, 2 and 4 h. To further confirm the intracellular •OH, the A549 cells were further treated with 1 mM of TEMPO for 4 h as a control group. After three washes with the PBS solution, the cells were imaged with an inverted fluorescent microscope at 340-nm excitation wavelength.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and characterizations of the F-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To access structure and morphology of the F-CDs, different methods were adopted. The F-CDs have good dispersion and a nearly spherical geometry as shown by the TEM image (Fig. 1 a). Most particles own a uniform particle size with a mean diameter of 5.2 nm. The distribution range of the F-CDs is narrow, that is, from 2.7 nm to 6.9 nm, as further confirmed by DLS (inset Fig. 1a). The HRTEM image of the F-CDs clearly presents an average lattice spacing (0.21 nm) (Fig. 1b), suggesting that the F-CDs have a crystalline nature [43]. Moreover, the XRD spectrum of the F-CDs shows a diffraction peak value at 2θ = 23.2° (Fig. 1c), with a relatively broad peak at the (002) planes, indicating carbon atoms with no specific structure and confirming the graphitic nature [43]. The FTIR spectrum of the as-appeared F-CDs was obtained to analyse their surface groups (Fig. 1d). The strong and wide band from 3419 cm−1 to 3550 cm−1 belongs to –OH which is mainly caused by the stretching vibration of the polysaccharide glycoside hydroxyl. There is a weak peak at 2900 cm−1 associated with the stretching vibration of CH in the sugar ring, including CH, CH2, and CH3 [44]. The band around 1392 cm−1 indicates the existence of CH bond. There is a peak at 1645 cm−1, due to the stretching vibration of CO in Amide I which might have originated from the conjugated protein of the polysaccharides, which are the main components of F. velutipes [45]. Additionally, the sharp band around 1132 cm−1 is derived from the stretching vibrations of the pyranose monomers of the polysaccharides, and the obvious band around 603 cm−1 is from skeletal modes of the pyranose rings [38,45]. The above two bands conform to the typical FTIR characteristic bands of polysaccharides.

The chemical compositions of the F-CDs were also analysed by XPS. The XPS spectrum (Fig. 1e) performs three obvious peaks at 284 eV (C 1s), 399 eV (N 1s) and 531 eV (O 1s). The elemental composition of the F-CDs contains oxygen (53 %), carbon (33 %) and nitrogen (12 %), indicating that the F-CDs were doped with the heteroatoms from the amino acids and proteins present in F. velutipes. The deconvolution of the C 1s spectrum (Fig. 1f) is regarded as three main peaks at 285.1, 286.4 and 288.5 eV, belonging to CC, CO and CO groups, respectively. The deconvolution of the O 1s peak (Fig. 1g) exhibits three peaks at 531.6, 532.1, and 536.2 eV for the CO, C − OH and COC groups, respectively. The N 1s peak (Fig. 1h) also consists of pyridinic and pyrrolic nitrogen at 399.3 and 400.4 eV, respectively, which is consistent with the FTIR results [46]. These results indicate that the polysaccharides’ structure and some organic groups, peculiarly OH, NH and CO, are confirmed on the prepared F-CDs surface. Numerous studies have shown that the polysaccharides can act as electron or hydrogen donors with antioxidant activities and as a result of the reaction between the polysaccharides and radicals, non-toxic products are produced [38,47]. This finding implies that F-CDs may have potential in monitoring the ROS in living cells.

As-prepared F-CDs were analyzed with UV–vis and PL spectra to determine their optical properties. The UV–vis spectrum (Fig. 2 a) of the F-CDs shows an absorption peak at ∼ 260 nm (black line) owing to the π-π* transition from the sp2 domain (CC) [48]. Bright blue fluorescence emission was obtained (blue line) under 380-nm excitation, as observed under irradiation with a 365-nm UV lamp (inset images). The quantum yield (QY) of the F-CDs was calculated to be ∼ 13 % with quinine sulphate as a reference. The PL spectra of the F-CDs were measured (Fig. 2b). In accordance with the previously reported CDs, after a change in excitation wavelengths, the fluorescence intensity increased before gradually decreasing. All of these emission bands are concentrated at the 400–550 nm wavelength range, and the strongest fluorescence emission is at the excitation wavelength of 380 nm. Moreover, the unmoved emission peak at 450 nm shows the excitation-independent emission characteristic of the F-CDs which is relevant to the surface states/defects emission [49]. Additionally, the stability of the F-CDs was further researched at different pH values (Figure S1).

Considering a complex biological system for the detection of the •OH, the interference of different metal ions and ROS should be clarified. The fluorescence responses of the F-CDs to 1 mM of different metal ions (Fe2+, Cu2+, K+, Ca2+, Mn2+ and Ni2+) and ROS (TBHP, ClO-, H2O2, 1O2 and •OH) were experimented on to evaluate the selectivity (Fig. 3 a). Relative to the other substances that may be present in living cells at the same concentration, the •OH has a striking fluorescence quenching influence on the F-CDs, and the fluorescence intensity decreases to approximately 10 % of its original intensity after the introduction of the •OH. Except for the •OH, the other substances do not trigger significant fluorescence changes.

For further confirmation, the Fenton reaction system was used to study the relationship between the fluorescence intensity of the F-CDs and the •OH concentrations in a PBS buffer (pH = 7.4) (Fig. 3b). The Fe2+-EDTA chelate could prevent the possible precipitation of Fe2+ [2]. The comparison of the fluorescence signals of the F-CDs indicates no obvious variations in fluorescence intensities from different systems (F-CDs + H2O2 or F-CDs + Fe2+-EDTA). However, there appeared to be a decrease in fluorescence intensity of F-CDs in the presence of Fe2+-EDTA and H2O2 due to the •OH generated by the Fenton reagent, suggesting a significant relationship between the F-CDs and the •OH. The effects of the reaction time between the F-CDs and the •OH were investigated first (Figure S2), suggesting the detection process of the F-CDs for the •OH required a 10-min incubation step. Subsequently, the PL spectra of the as-appeared F-CDs were obtained after adding the •OH at different concentrations under optimised condition (Fig. 3c). Based on the above results, the F-CDs appear to be a promising probe for quantitative •OH assay with high sensitivity and selectivity.

As expected, the intensity of the F-CDs characteristic peak at approximately 450 nm decreased greatly with the increasing •OH concentration. The fluorescence intensity ratio ((F0-F) / F0) increased linearly with the •OH concentration from 0 to 12 μM (y = 0.1095x + 0.0661, R2 = 0.996), with a detection limit (LOD) as low as 95 nM (based on a signal-to-noise ratio S/N = 3) (Fig. 3d), which is comparable with the values obtained using other reported fluorescence sensors for the •OH, as shown in Table 1 .

The fluorescence lifetime was further obtained to understand the fluorescence quenching mechanism of the F-CDs (Fig. 4 a). They possess a shorter fluorescence lifetime (5.35 ns) in the presence of the •OH than those without the •OH (6.41 ns) at the 405-nm excitation wavelength. The shortening of lifetime may be due to the energy transfer between the F-CDs and the •OH which induces the oxidation of the electron-donating groups (OH, NH and CO, etc.) [24]. Additionally, the UV–vis assay of the F-CDs towards the •OH was performed (Fig. 4b). With the increasing •OH concentration from 0 to 12 μM, the UV absorption peak of 273 nm gradually strengthened.

The CV experiments were employed to study the redox behaviour between the F-CDs and the •OH (Fig. 4c). There was a noticeable reduction peak at −0.184 V vs Ag/AgCl on the F-CDs modified electrode after treated with the •OH. In order to get rid of Fe2+ or H2O2, the reduction currents of the F-CDs modified electrode treated with Fe2+ (1 mM) or H2O2 (6 mM) were recorded as the control experiments. And there were no significant changes in the reduction currents of the above control groups. These phenomena indicate that the F-CDs can be oxidized by the •OH as the electron donor because of the abundant groups (OH, NH and CO, etc.) on the F-CDs surface [57,58] Together with the lifetime and UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 4a and 4b), we suggest that the oxidation reaction between the F-CDs and the •OH triggers the fluorescence quenching of the F-CDs [47–59].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of the •OH in living cells
ce:section.ce:para: In order to assess their biocompatibility and feasibility for further biological applications, especially in intracellular imaging, the cytotoxicity of the F-CDs was determined. The cell viability of the A549 cells (a human lung cancer cell line) and the HepG2 cells (a human liver cancer cell line) was determined with the increasing concentration of the F-CDs for 24 h by a CCK-8 assay, separately (Fig. 5 ). From the 0–50 μg/mL range of the F-CDs, the survival rates of either cell remained over 90 %, indicating that the F-CDs have good biocompatibility and could be further developed in cell imaging.

As a fluorescent probe, the F-CDs were used to monitor the level of the •OH in the A549 cells. The cells were pretreated without the F-CDs have no fluorescence (Figure S3). After pre-treating the A549 cells with 20 μg/mL of the F-CDs for 24 h, the cells show blue fluorescence at the 340-nm excitation wavelength, suggesting that the F-CDs own a good imaging capacity for cells in vitro (Fig. 6 a). To monitor the •OH, the A549 cells were pretreated with the F-CDs (20 μg/mL) for 24 h and treated with various concentrations of Fe2+ with 1 mM H2O2 (•OH) for 30 min. The results showed that the fluorescence signals of the cells became weaker and weaker with increasing concentrations of the •OH, suggesting that the intake level of the •OH in the cells increased (Fig. 6b–6d).

To further verify the capability of monitoring the endogenous •OH in biological application, four groups of the A549 cells were designed using the F-CDs as effective tools for testing (Fig. 7 ). As a stimulator of cell respiratory burst, PMA could cause ROS over-production to increase the •OH concentration in the cells. [60,61]. The A549 cells were stimulated by the PMA during different time periods (0, 2 and 4 h), and then the fluorescence signals weakened dramatically with the increasing •OH due to the interaction between the F-CDs and the •OH produced by the PMA (Fig. 7a–7c).

Significant fluorescence decreases from 1.0 to 0.3 were observed (Fig. 7d). A decrease in intracellular concentrations of the •OH was observed by introducing TEMPO, the most used radical scavenger, realising the controlled change of the concentration levels of the intracellular •OH [25]. To further determine that the above fluorescence quenching originated from the •OH, the A549 cells were incubated with TEMPO for 1 h before stimulation by the PMA as a control group, and a remarkable enhancement in fluorescence intensity was observed (Fig. 7d).

In summary, a kind of biomass CDs was synthesised as •OH-responsive fluorescent sensors from the polysaccharide-rich precursor of F. velutipes using a simple one-step hydrothermal process without any additional modification. The F-CDs have excellent sensitivity to and selectivity for the interference of different metal ions and ROS, and it exists a good linear detection range from 0 to 12 μM with an extremely low LOD of 95 nM to detect the •OH. Additionally, the F-CDs exhibit excitation-independent emission, favourable biocompatibility and low toxicity, making them useful for applications under physiological conditions. For the intracellular •OH concentration levels, the F-CDs are successfully used to monitor the PMA-stimulated •OH generation in the A549 cells. Given the combined benefits of the green and eco-friendly approach, the biomass-derived CDs are expected to have considerable potential as a novel theranostics tool for early detection and treatment of related diseases.

Yu Hou: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Resources, Writing – original draft, Funding acquisition. Jiawei Lin: Data curation, Software, Investigation. Yichao Chen: Data curation, Software, Investigation. Jianbin He: Data curation, Investigation, Validation. Zhe Su: Data curation, Validation. Qinhua Zheng: Investigation, Validation, Visualization. Xiujuan Zhao: Resources, Funding acquisition. Xiaodan Lv: Resources, Funding acquisition. Xiuping Tang: Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition. Changren Zhou: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The study was supported by the Innovative School Project of Education Department of Guangdong (No. 2018KTSCX305, 2021KQNCX145, and 2022KTSCX187), the Scientific and Technological Innovation Strategy of Guangdong Province (“Climbing Plan”) Special Fund Project in 2022 (No. pdjh2022b0723), the Key Construction Academic Subject for Scientific Research Quality Improvement of Guangdong Province (No. 2021ZDJS139) and Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Fund Regional Joint Youth Fund (No. 2019A515110262).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2022.122304.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Rhodamine cyclic hydrazide as a fluorescent probe for the detection of hydroxyl radicals
Chem. Commun.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Kim, S. Ko, H. Kim, I. Shin, J. Tae, Rhodamine cyclic hydrazide as a fluorescent probe for the detection of hydroxyl radicals. Chem. Commun. 49 (2013) 7959−7961.
